narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid LSAT score and upper-middle family income background. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 48% chance of failure for you, which crossed the threshold to reject your application—meaning the school sees too high a risk that you might not pass right away. This prediction was driven primarily by your Year 3 school decile ranking, which was quite low at 1 compared to the typical student's 5.5, strongly pushing the model toward expecting bar exam struggles. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 36, close to the average of 36.7, worked significantly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.6, below the usual 3.2, also helped a bit by nudging things positively, though not as strongly. Factors like your upper-middle family income and full-time student status provided smaller boosts toward passing, while your Year 1 decile of 4 was only a minor influence overall. Compared to typical students, your later academic performance stood out as the biggest red flag, dipping well below average, even as your test scores held steady. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman leader in law—many overcome similar hurdles—but the model weighs these patterns heavily to protect students from bar exam setbacks. To move forward, consider retaking courses or boosting your senior-year grades to improve your decile rankings, perhaps seeking targeted LSAT or GPA prep programs tailored for underrepresented students like you. You might also explore part-time options or schools with stronger support for first-generation or diverse applicants. Reapply after addressing these areas, and don't lose hope—your story is one of resilience, and with focused steps, you can shift the odds. We're rooting for you.
